fear_greed_sentiment: Extreme Fear, up slightly from 18.8 prior

posts.author: Boredom Baron
posts.title: The Wellness Economy's Best-Kept Secret: A 75-Year-Old Finnish Compounder With 24% ROE
posts.summary: A deep industry primer on the global sauna equipment market ($900M, growing to $1.56B by 2033 at 6.3% CAGR), situated within the broader $6.8T wellness economy. The free section educates on three demand drivers: rising home sauna penetration outside the Nordics, a commercial hospitality boom (Marriott 260+ luxury properties), and infrared sauna growth at 7.5% CAGR. The competitive landscape is consolidating — Kohler acquired KLAFS (Germany) and Masco (MAS) acquired Sauna360 for ~€130M. The author teases a Finnish pure-play publicly traded company commanding 20%+ global market share with 24% ROE, 40-50% gross margins, and factories in seven countries. The specific ticker and full financial analysis are behind the paywall.

posts.author: Pippa's Pen & Podcast
posts.title: Hormuz Replaced by Stars in a Box, Atoms and Light
posts.summary: Dr. Pippa Malmgren explores the geopolitical implications of the US bypassing traditional energy chokepoints — specifically Kharg Island and the Strait of Hormuz — through advanced energy technologies. The thesis centers on how nuclear fusion ("a star in a box"), advanced nuclear, and photonic energy systems are rendering the traditional "blockade of molecules" strategy obsolete. This has profound implications for energy geopolitics, the petrodollar system, and nations whose leverage depends on controlling hydrocarbon transit routes. The piece frames this as a structural shift from a molecule-based to an atom-and-light-based energy paradigm.

posts.author: Cassandra Unchained (Michael Burry)
posts.title: The Toxic Twins Recurrence: Fannie Mae & Freddie Mac
posts.summary: Michael Burry reveals he has been buying both Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac common stock "down here," framing it as a classic value investing scenario where the pain of holding through a falling knife is precisely what creates the opportunity. The subtitle reads "Stocks Fall Hard into the News Gap." Burry draws on historical market parallels, noting that the most spectacular recoveries in market history required "absolutely inhuman" conviction to hold through the drawdown. The full analysis is behind a paywall, but the free preview confirms active accumulation of both GSE stocks. This is a significant signal given Burry's track record and the ongoing political debate around GSE privatization.

posts.author: Nate's Substack
posts.title: Your AI credentials don't matter. Your artifacts do. Here's how to build both sides — plus I'm launching Nate's Network.
posts.summary: Nate argues there are "essentially infinite AI jobs right now" — every company he works with has uncapped AI talent budgets and still can't fill roles. He analyzed hundreds of real job postings at Anthropic, Robinhood, Upwork, Glean, and Scale AI and identified seven specific, learnable skills that consistently appear across AI roles paying $150K-$400K. The key insight is a "K-shaped split" in the labor market: infinite demand at the top, unemployment at the bottom. He argues most AI training is taught at the wrong altitude — either too high-level ("AI for Everyone") or too deep (ML engineering) — while the critical middle layer barely exists. DataCamp found 40% of AI training is just video courses, and 23% of leaders say it doesn't translate to real work. Nate is launching "Nate's Network," a vetted AI talent network matching people to roles based on what they've actually built, not credentials.

posts.author: The Price of Everything
posts.title: How to pick a wealth manager?
posts.summary: Tim Price shares a video interview with James Faulkner of "Poor Millionaires" discussing how to evaluate and select a wealth manager. The embedded YouTube video is titled "3 things you must ask your wealth manager." This is a short-form media post (video interview) rather than a written analysis. The content focuses on practical wealth management selection criteria for individual investors.

posts.author: The Founders Corner
posts.title: The Silent Killer Inside Your Company — And Why You Put It There
posts.summary: Chris Tottman explores micromanagement as the "silent killer" inside scaling companies, arguing it stems from fear and perfectionism rather than ego. The post identifies three pillars for breaking the cycle: (1) true delegation (transferring ownership, not just tasks), (2) structured alignment rhythms (weekly reviews, milestone check-ins) instead of hovering, and (3) outcome-based accountability rather than process control. Key insight: "The more control you relinquish strategically, the more control you actually gain over outcomes." Also promotes the launch of "The Big Book of BrainDumps," a founder's field manual with 70 frameworks from 30+ years of scaling software companies.

posts.author: Wolf of Oakville
posts.title: Draganfly Inc ($DPRO/$DPRO.C) FINS Review
posts.summary: A scathing FINS review of Draganfly, a dual-listed drone technology company. Despite operating in a "hotly themed sector," the financials are deeply problematic. Key findings: (1) Revenue of $7.7M in 2025 (+18% YoY) but gross margin collapsed to 17.1% (-400bps); (2) Net loss of $23M, 66% worse than prior year; (3) Q4 operational cash burn of $9.7M ($39M annualized); (4) 440% share dilution in 12 months (now ~570% since start of last year); (5) Break-even would require $134.5M in revenue — they achieved 5.7% of that; (6) ROIC at -45%; (7) Market cap ~$250M CAD is 32x 2025 revenue; (8) Insiders flipping RSU awards on open market. The only positive: ~$150M cash on hand after multiple capital raises, providing a long runway. Wolf gave it 1.5/5 stars, calling out management's poor capital allocation and the "stupidity of investors" who participated in recent raises (now down 28%).
